1: Jericho is identified with Tell es-Sultan, a mound at the spring of Ain es-Sultan in the Jordan Valley just north of the Dead Sea. It is one of the oldest continuously occupied sites in the world, with occupation layers going back to the Neolithic (ca. 9000 BCE). The archaeological debate about the Late Bronze Age city is significant: John Garstang (excavated 1930–1936) identified a destruction layer (City IV, his “City D”) with a collapsed outer wall dated to ca. 1400 BCE, consistent with the early exodus date (1446 BCE). Kathleen Kenyon (1952–1958) re-examined the site and argued the LBA destruction Garstang found was actually MB IIC (ca. 1550 BCE), with no significant LBA occupation present — creating the apparent archaeological problem that there was no city to conquer in the late LBA. More recent analysis (Bryant Wood, 1990) re-dates Kenyon's City IV destruction back to ca. 1400 BCE using carbon-14 and ceramic evidence, reopening the debate. The scarcity of Late Bronze remains at Tell es-Sultan may reflect Jericho's status as a small, lightly occupied administrative post rather than a major fortified city — consistent with the narrative's emphasis on the walls as the only military feature.
2: YHWH's opening assurance — “See, I have given Jericho into your hand” — uses the prophetic perfect (the future act stated as already accomplished from the divine perspective), a standard feature of holy-war rhetoric in which YHWH's prior gift of victory precedes the human act of taking it. The same formula appears in Deuteronomy's conquest speeches (Deuteronomy 2:24; Joshua 7:2; Joshua 20:13) and reflects a theological claim: Israel is not conquering Canaan by military superiority but executing a divine decree already enacted in the heavenly realm. The ANE parallel is the Assyrian and Hittite royal annals, where the storm-god gives the king his enemies before the battle — but the Israelite version differs in that there is no elaborate ritual to secure the god's favor; the gift is unconditional and prior.
3: The seven-day march with seven priests carrying seven rams’ horn trumpets (shofar hayovel) has the structure of a liturgical procession rather than a military siege. The hayovel (Jubilee horn) directly connects to Leviticus 25: the Jubilee year was announced by the blowing of the shofar hayovel on the Day of Atonement, releasing all debt-slaves and restoring all ancestral land. Jericho, as the first city taken, functions as the “Jubilee city” — its conquest inaugurates the land redistribution to Israel's tribes. The seven-fold structure (seven days, seven priests, seven trumpets, seventh day seven times) is patterned on creation-week: the completion of the conquest, like the completion of creation, is on the seventh. The number seven also signals holy completeness — the city falls not through human engineering but through divine decree at the appointed fullness of time.
4: The ark of the covenant's placement at the center of the procession signals that it is YHWH's march, not Joshua's. In the wilderness narratives, the ark led Israel's movements (Numbers 10:33-36); at the Jordan crossing the ark went ahead of the people (Joshua 3:6). The procession's order — armed men in front, priests with trumpets, ark, rear guard — mirrors the arrangement of the tabernacle camp (Num 2) and the wilderness march, suggesting that the conquest is a continuation of the wilderness journey, not a departure from it. The ark's presence transforms the military action into a liturgical act: YHWH is coming to take possession of his land, and Israel is his processional retinue.
5: The command to shout (rua) when the trumpets blast is a technical term in holy war: teru'ah is the battle cry and the blast of the ram's horn simultaneously. In Leviticus 23:24 the same term marks the Feast of Trumpets (Rosh Hashanah), suggesting an overlap between the cultic calendar and the conquest narrative. In Numbers 23:21, Balaam observes “the shout of a king is among them” (teru'at melek) — YHWH's presence with Israel expressed through the battle shout. The collapse of the walls at the teru'ah is the narrative's way of saying: the conquest was YHWH's direct act, not Israel's military achievement, and the mechanism was the presence of the divine king in his processional ark.
6: The seven rams’ horn trumpets (shivah shofarot hayovelim) are specifically Jubilee instruments. The LXX renders hayovel as salpigges (trumpets), losing the Jubilee connection, but the Hebrew is precise. The shofar hayovel was the instrument of release, restoration, and liberty — its use in the conquest of Jericho signals that Israel is entering their inheritance, not merely taking territory. The priests are not soldiers; their role is to carry the instruments of divine declaration. The military men in front and behind are essentially a liturgical escort for the real agent of the siege: the divine presence in the ark, declared by the Jubilee trumpets.
7: The instruction “do not shout, let no word come out of your mouth” for the first six days creates a sustained liturgical silence. This is the silence before the divine act — the same silence commanded before holy-war encounters (Exodus 14:14: “YHWH will fight for you; you need only be still”). The silence emphasizes that Israel's part is not military action but disciplined participation in YHWH's procession. The contrast with the shout on day seven is deliberate: six days of silence followed by one commanded shout mirrors the creation week structure and heightens the theological point that the seventh day is the day of divine completion.
8: Joshua's command to the people on day one — march, do not shout, do not speak — positions the conquest as an act of liturgical discipline. Military discipline and cultic discipline are identical in the holy-war framework. The Israelites are a holy army precisely because they are YHWH's processional retinue; their discipline is the discipline of approaching the divine presence, not merely the discipline of a well-ordered military force. This theological framing of the army as a liturgical procession is unique to Israel in the ANE and reflects the covenant conception of war as enacted worship.
9: The arrangement of the procession — armed vanguard, priests with trumpets, ark, armed rear guard — reflects both military and liturgical order. The Hebrew term for the rear guard (measef) means “gatherer” or “rear assembly,” used also in Isaiah 52:12: “YHWH will go before you, and the God of Israel will be your rear guard.” The procession around Jericho is thus a miniature of the exodus journey: divine presence leading, divine presence protecting from behind, the people in the middle walking in covenant order.
10: The silence command is reinforced here: “you shall not shout or make your voice heard, neither shall any word go out of your mouth.” The phrase “no word go out of your mouth” is the antithesis of the teru'ah to come — all speech is reserved for the commanded moment. This restraint on human speech parallels the restraint on human military initiative: YHWH alone acts, Israel alone obeys. The liturgical silence is the form of their obedience.
11: The ark of YHWH goes around the city once — the beginning of the seven-day circuit. The circuit is not a military inspection but a territorial claim: YHWH walking the perimeter of the city he is claiming. In ANE boundary-walking rituals, deities or kings walked the boundaries of territory to assert ownership (cf. the treaty texts from Ugarit and the Hittite archives where divine-witness tours established territorial grants). YHWH's circuit of Jericho is the assertion of his sovereign claim before the visible judgment falls.
12: On the second day they rose early — the same verb (hashkem) used of Abraham rising early to go to Moriah (Genesis 22:3) and of Moses rising early at Sinai (Exodus 34:4). The early rising signals eager obedience; the holy-war context makes each early rising a recommitment to the task. The repetition through seven days also structures the narrative as a week, with each day building liturgical pressure toward the seventh.
13: The seven priests, seven trumpets, ark, and procession repeat exactly — the narrative deliberately slows down to emphasize the daily repetition. Biblical scholars note the “repetitive resumption” (Wiederaufnahme) technique here: the repetition is not literary redundancy but a narrative device signaling covenantal regularity. Each circuit is a liturgical enactment of the same divine claim; the repetition is the form of Israel's obedience and YHWH's patient assertion.
14: Six days of single circuits establish the pattern. The number six signals incompleteness in the biblical symbolic system — six days of creation work before the seventh-day rest. The six circuits achieve nothing visible in military terms; their purpose is entirely theological: to establish that the seventh circuit, with its sevenfold intensity, is the divine completion. Jericho does not fall through attrition or military pressure over six days; it falls through divine act on the seventh day at the moment of completion.
15: On the seventh day they rose early at dawn (ba'alot hashachar) and circled seven times — the only day with seven circuits and the only day with specified dawn timing. The dawn rising connects to the theophanic dawn traditions (Exodus 14:24: YHWH in the pillar of fire looked down at dawn and threw Egypt into confusion) and the Passover imagery (Israel left Egypt at night; the Egyptian army was destroyed at dawn). The seventh day of the Jericho siege is structurally analogous to the Passover night: the night of seven circuits followed by the dawn destruction.
16: On the seventh circuit of the seventh day the priests blow the trumpets and Joshua commands the shout — “for YHWH has given you the city.” The prophetic perfect from verse 2 is now confirmed: what YHWH declared already accomplished at the beginning is now enacted visibly. The shout (teru'ah) is the human response to the divine declaration, not the cause of the wall's collapse. Theologically, the sequence is: divine decree → human obedience → divine fulfillment, with no human military action in the middle.
17: The cherem (devoted/banned thing) declared over Jericho is the book's central concept for the first conquered city. Cherem is from a root meaning to separate, set apart, or prohibit; it covers a spectrum from “devoted to YHWH's use” (the metals going to the sanctuary treasury, v19) to “devoted to destruction” (everything else). The Mesha Stele (ca. 840 BCE), inscribed by King Mesha of Moab, uses the same term: “I devoted it [Israel's garrison at Nebo] to Ashtar-Chemosh,” indicating that cherem was an ANE holy-war concept applied to the defeated as a gift to the victorious deity. What distinguishes the Israelite cherem is that it is commanded by YHWH as a theological quarantine — Canaanite religious practice is not to survive to corrupt Israel. Rahab and her household are explicitly excluded from the cherem because she had aligned herself with YHWH's purposes (v25).
18: The warning against taking anything from the cherem is framed in terms of contamination: “lest when you have devoted [cheramtem] it you take any of the devoted things [cherem] and make the camp of Israel a thing devoted [cherem] and bring trouble upon it.” The logic is contagion: the cherem status is transferable; if anyone takes cherem items, they become cherem themselves and bring that status onto the whole camp. This is the narrative setup for the Achan episode in chapter 7, where exactly this contamination logic is worked out. The cherem is not merely a military policy but a theological fence maintaining the purity of Israel's covenant relationship.
19: All silver and gold and vessels of bronze and iron are holy to YHWH and go into his treasury. This is the firstfruits principle applied to the first city: Jericho, as the first conquered city, belongs entirely to YHWH as the firstfruits of the land's produce belong to him. The metals — valuable and durable — go to the sanctuary treasury (the same logic as dedicating the firstfruits of the harvest). Everything else is destroyed. The distinction is between what can be purified and incorporated into YHWH's service (metals can be refined) and what cannot (living things contaminated by Canaanite practice). The sanctuary treasury (in later tradition, the bronze and iron vessels become the foundation of the temple treasury, 1 Kings 7:51) thus contains the firstfruits of the conquest.
20: The wall collapsed (vatipol hachomah tachtehah) and the people went up straight ahead. No mechanism is described — neither earthquake, mining, nor battering ram. The narrative presents the collapse as the direct result of the shout (and behind the shout, the divine declaration of verse 2). Archaeological note: the walls at Tell es-Sultan show evidence of a catastrophic collapse in the relevant destruction layer — Garstang and Wood describe the outer revetment wall falling outward onto the base, creating a ramped approach of rubble up which attackers could walk. The phrase “went up straight ahead” is consistent with this: no gate assault, but a broad-front entry over the collapsed debris.
21: The cherem of all living things (men and women, young and old, ox and sheep and donkey) is stated matter-of-factly. The thoroughness of the command reflects the theological logic of the cherem: partial execution creates the contamination it was meant to prevent (as Saul's partial cherem of the Amalekites in 1 Sam 15 demonstrates, with Samuel's subsequent rebuke). The moral weight of this text has exercised biblical interpreters from Origen (who allegorized it as the soul's conquest of its passions) to modern OT scholars (who situate it in the ANE genre of holy-war rhetoric, noting that Mesha's Moabite Stele makes similar total-destruction claims that archaeology does not confirm literally). The narrative's theological point is not a general warrant for violence but a specific act of divine judgment on cities whose sin had reached the point of no return (Genesis 15:16: “the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete”).
22: Joshua's dispatch of the two spies to bring out Rahab fulfills the oath sworn in chapter 2. The spies had sworn by their lives (nafshotenu tachat motkhem, “our lives for yours”) — a solemn oath formula that creates a binding obligation. Rahab's rescue is the narrative embodiment of the chesed principle: the loyal love shown to the spies is repaid by loyal love to her household. The inclusion of her “father, mother, brothers, and all who belonged to her” reflects the household-solidarity principle of covenant: the faith of one head of household can cover the whole family (as Rahab functioned as a de facto household head in sheltering the spies).
23: The burning of the city and everything in it completes the cherem. Fire is the standard medium of cherem destruction (Deuteronomy 13:16 commands burning a city that has gone after other gods). The metals are exempted because fire purifies them rather than destroying them — consistent with the purification logic of Numbers 31:22-23, where metals taken from Midian must pass through fire for purification. The burning of Jericho is the visual enactment of offering the city to YHWH: the smoke ascending is the sacrifice of the first city, the firstfruits offering of the conquest.
24: Joshua pronounces a rebuilding curse on Jericho: whoever rebuilds it “shall lay its foundation at the cost of his firstborn, and at the cost of his youngest shall he set up its gates.” This curse is fulfilled literally in 1 Kings 16:34 — Hiel of Bethel rebuilt Jericho in the time of Ahab, “he laid its foundation at the cost of Abiram his firstborn, and set up its gates at the cost of his youngest son Segub, according to the word of YHWH that he spoke by Joshua.” The curse-fulfillment is one of the clearest examples in Kings of an earlier prophetic word being literally enacted; it also frames the Jericho destruction as permanently significant — the city is to remain a warning monument, not a site to be reclaimed for human use.
25: Rahab the prostitute, her father's household, and all who belonged to her were saved — “she has lived in Israel to this day” is the formulaic phrase signaling continuing presence and integration. The cherem exception for Rahab is theologically significant: the ban is not ethnic (not “all Canaanites must die”) but theological (all who are given to YHWH's enemies must die; all who align with YHWH's purposes may live). Rahab is the narrative proof that the cherem is not genocide but judgment — distinguishing those who resist YHWH from those who submit. Matthew 1:5 places Rahab in the Davidic/Messianic genealogy, and Hebrews 11:31 and James 2:25 cite her as a model of faith, making the “Gentile prostitute saved from cherem” one of the Bible's central illustrations of grace operating outside ethnic covenant membership.
26: Joshua's curse in verse 26 is reported as a public declaration, and verse 26 closes with “so YHWH was with Joshua, and his fame was in all the land.” The curse functions as a prophetic word: Joshua speaks and YHWH fulfills (cf. 1 Kings 16:34). The tikvah (hope/cord) wordplay from Rahab's scarlet thread finds its shadow here in the cursed city: the city of the scarlet cord's hope is placed under a rebuilding ban so that no one can mistake Jericho for a permanent human achievement. The contrast between the saved household and the cursed ruins is the theological summary of the chapter: YHWH saves the faithful and destroys the resistant, and his word — whether of salvation or judgment — does not fail.
27: YHWH was with Joshua and his fame (shim'o, his report/reputation) spread throughout all the land. This verse functions as a summary acclamation after the first major military-theological event. The spread of Joshua's fame is not ego-building but missional: Rahab had reported in chapter 2 that “all the inhabitants of the land melt before you,” and the Jericho event confirms that report throughout Canaan. The fear of YHWH — mediated through the fame of his servant — is itself a mode of conquest: it breaks the morale of the remaining cities before a single additional battle. This prepares the reader for the Gibeonite deception in chapter 9, where fear of YHWH's reputation drives the deception.
